meta.name: SIMD Optimization Library
meta.description: SSE/AVX intrinsics for memcpy, string search, and vector math
meta.essence: Data-parallel computation using CPU vector registers to process multiple values simultaneously, requiring explicit memory alignment, safe memory access patterns, and understanding of instruction-level parallelism to achieve significant performance gains over scalar code.
meta.why_important: SIMD optimization is critical for performance-sensitive applications and teaches low-level performance engineering skills that separate competent programmers from systems experts who can extract maximum hardware efficiency.
learning_outcomes: Implement memory operations using 128-bit and 256-bit vector registers with proper alignment
Write intrinsic functions mapping directly to machine instructions
Design safe memory access patterns that avoid page-fault hazards
Benchmark SIMD code rigorously against scalar implementations
Analyze compiler auto-vectorization and identify when hand-written SIMD wins
Debug alignment faults and understand memory boundary requirements
Implement horizontal reduction operations efficiently using shuffle patterns

milestones.name: SSE2 Basics: memset and memcpy
milestones.description: Implement basic memory operations using SSE2 128-bit registers.
acceptance_criteria: Implement simd_memset using _mm_store_si128 processing 16 bytes per iteration for aligned regions
Implement simd_memcpy using _mm_load_si128 / _mm_store_si128 pairs for aligned 16-byte copies
Handle unaligned buffer edges with a scalar prologue (process bytes until 16-byte aligned) and scalar epilogue (process remaining bytes after last full 16-byte chunk)
For buffers smaller than 16 bytes, fall back entirely to scalar implementation
Benchmark against libc memset/memcpy and scalar loop across buffer sizes (64B, 1KB, 64KB, 1MB, 16MB)
Show measurable speedup over naive scalar loop for buffers >= 1KB; explain results where no speedup is observed (libc is already SIMD-optimized)
Use _mm_stream_si128 (non-temporal store) for buffers larger than L2 cache and benchmark the difference
pitfalls: _mm_store_si128 requires 16-byte aligned pointers; use _mm_storeu_si128 for unaligned, but prefer aligning the pointer
Compiler may auto-vectorize scalar code, making benchmarks unfair; use __attribute__((optimize("no-tree-vectorize"))) or volatile for scalar baseline
libc memset/memcpy are heavily optimized (often using AVX+non-temporal stores); you're competing against expert-written code
Forgetting the scalar prologue/epilogue causes segfaults on unaligned inputs
deliverables: SIMD memset with alignment handling and scalar fallback
SIMD memcpy with alignment handling and scalar fallback
Non-temporal store variant for large buffers
Benchmark suite with fair scalar baseline (auto-vectorization disabled)

milestones.name: String Operations: strlen and memchr
milestones.description: Implement SIMD-optimized string scanning with safe memory access patterns.
acceptance_criteria: Implement simd_strlen using _mm_cmpeq_epi8 to compare 16 bytes against zero simultaneously
Implement simd_memchr using _mm_cmpeq_epi8 to find a target byte across 16 bytes simultaneously
Use _mm_movemask_epi8 to convert comparison results to a 16-bit bitmask; use __builtin_ctz (count trailing zeros) to find the first match position

Handle page-boundary safety: before the first aligned read, compute the distance from the input pointer to the next page boundary (4096-byte aligned). If the input is within 16 bytes of a page boundary, use scalar scanning until aligned. After alignment, 16-byte aligned reads within a page cannot fault.
For the initial unaligned chunk (pointer not 16-byte aligned), either use scalar scanning OR read from the aligned-down address and mask out bytes before the actual start
Verify correctness with strings of lengths 0, 1, 15, 16, 17, 4095, 4096, 4097 (page boundary cases)
Benchmark against libc strlen/memchr across string lengths
pitfalls: CRITICAL: Reading 16 bytes starting at an arbitrary address can cross a page boundary into unmapped memory, causing a segfault. Always align reads to 16-byte boundaries; an aligned 16-byte read that starts within a mapped page cannot cross a page boundary.
When reading from an aligned-down address, the first few bytes may be before the string start; mask these out of the movemask result to avoid false positives
movemask returns a 16-bit value where bit N corresponds to byte N; __builtin_ctz gives the index of the first set bit
For memchr with a size limit, must not scan past the end of the buffer
deliverables: SIMD strlen with page-safe aligned reads
SIMD memchr with page-safe aligned reads
Correctness test suite including page-boundary edge cases

milestones.name: Math Operations: Dot Product and Matrix Multiply
concepts: Efficient horizontal reduction via shuffle+add (NOT hadd)
acceptance_criteria: Implement SIMD dot product using _mm_mul_ps for element-wise multiply, then reduce using shuffle+add pattern (_mm_shuffle_ps + _mm_add_ps), NOT _mm_hadd_ps
Implement 4x4 float matrix multiplication using SSE, processing one row-column dot product per iteration
Support both row-major and column-major matrix layouts; benchmark both to demonstrate access pattern impact
Implement AVX (256-bit) variants of both operations using _mm256_* intrinsics
Insert _mm256_zeroupper() or use __attribute__((target("avx"))) to avoid AVX-SSE transition penalties when mixing code paths
Implement runtime CPU feature detection using CPUID (or compiler builtins) to select SSE or AVX code path
Benchmark scalar vs SSE vs AVX across array sizes (4, 16, 64, 256, 1024 elements for dot product; various matrix sizes)
Show at least 2x speedup for SSE and 3x for AVX over scalar for large inputs (1024+ elements)
pitfalls: _mm_hadd_ps has 3-5 cycle latency on many Intel CPUs; the shuffle+vertical-add pattern is faster for horizontal reduction
Row-major vs column-major layout drastically affects cache performance for matrix multiply; column-major allows vectorized column access
AVX-SSE transition penalty (up to ~70 cycles on older Intel) occurs when mixing 128-bit SSE and 256-bit AVX without vzeroupper
-ffast-math may be needed for the compiler to auto-vectorize floating-point reductions (it changes semantics); document tradeoffs
deliverables: SIMD dot product with shuffle+add horizontal reduction
4x4 matrix multiply with SSE and AVX variants
Runtime CPU feature detection for code path selection
vzeroupper handling for clean AVX-SSE transitions
Benchmark suite comparing scalar, SSE, AVX across data sizes

milestones.name: Auto-vectorization Analysis
milestones.description: Study compiler auto-vectorization and compare with hand-written SIMD.
acceptance_criteria: Write scalar versions of all functions structured for auto-vectorization (simple loops, no pointer aliasing, use restrict keyword)
Compile with -O3 -march=native -ftree-vectorize -fopt-info-vec-all (GCC) or -Rpass=loop-vectorize (Clang) and capture vectorization reports
Inspect generated assembly for at least 3 functions; annotate which SIMD instructions the compiler chose

Identify at least 2 cases where hand-written SIMD outperforms auto-vectorization and explain why (e.g., the compiler couldn't prove alignment, couldn't vectorize complex control flow, chose suboptimal instruction sequence)
Identify at least 1 case where auto-vectorization matches or beats hand-written SIMD and explain why
Benchmark methodology: pin CPU frequency (disable turbo boost and frequency scaling), warm up caches with 3 untimed runs, report median of 10+ timed runs with standard deviation
Disable auto-vectorization for scalar baselines using __attribute__((optimize("no-tree-vectorize"))) or equivalent
Produce a written analysis document (markdown) summarizing findings with assembly excerpts and benchmark data
pitfalls: CPU frequency scaling and turbo boost cause massive benchmark variance; pin frequency with cpupower or equivalent
Compiler may not vectorize loops with potential pointer aliasing; use restrict and __builtin_assume_aligned to help
-ffast-math enables FP reassociation which allows vectorization but changes numerical results; document when used
Micro-benchmarks on tiny data may be dominated by function call overhead, not the actual computation
Different compiler versions produce different vectorization decisions; record compiler version
deliverables: Annotated assembly excerpts for key functions
Comprehensive benchmark with statistical rigor (median, stddev, frequency pinning)

blueprint.title: SIMD Optimization Library
blueprint.overview: A hands-on exploration of Single Instruction Multiple Data (SIMD) programming, building a library of vectorized memory operations, string scanning, and mathematical computations. This project bridges the gap between high-level algorithmic thinking and the raw parallelism available in modern CPUs. You'll learn to think in terms of 128-bit and 256-bit vector registers, where one instruction processes 4, 8, or 16 data elements simultaneously. The journey progresses from fundamental SSE2 memory operations through string scanning with page-boundary safety, to floating-point math with horizontal reductions, culminating in a deep analysis of when hand-written SIMD beats compiler auto-vectorization—and when it doesn't.
blueprint.design_philosophy: SIMD is where software meets the physical reality of CPU execution units. The core tension is between the programmer's sequential mental model and the hardware's parallel nature. This project teaches you to see memory not as a stream of bytes but as aligned 16/32-byte chunks that map directly to XMM/YMM registers. Every milestone reinforces the three-level view: application APIs (memset, strlen, dot product), OS/hardware boundaries (page faults, cache lines), and silicon reality (execution ports, instruction latency, register file). You'll discover that SIMD performance isn't just about using vector instructions—it's about alignment, memory access patterns, cache behavior, and understanding what the compiler can and cannot do for you.

implementation.rationale: SIMD intrinsics are fundamentally C macros that map directly to machine instructions. C provides explicit control over memory layout, alignment, and pointer arithmetic—essential for correct SIMD code. The project requires understanding exactly what instructions execute, which is clearest in C with inline assembly inspection. No language abstraction layer should obscure the register-to-register operations.
implementation.style_guide: snake_case functions and variables; _simd suffix for SIMD variants; explicit alignment attributes on structs; restrict keyword on pointer parameters; target attribute for CPU feature-specific functions; comments explaining instruction latency/throughput for non-obvious patterns
implementation.build_system: Makefile with -march=native, separate targets for SSE2/AVX variants, benchmark runner with CPU frequency pinning
alternatives: Rust: Excellent SIMD support via portable_simd and core::arch, but adds abstraction layer that can obscure instruction mapping. Good for production, less ideal for learning raw intrinsics.
C++: Identical intrinsics to C, plus std::simd in C++26. Adds templates for generic vector widths, but same fundamental concepts.

assumed_known: Basic CPU concepts (registers, cache, memory hierarchy)
Understanding of arrays and memory layout

must_teach_first.concept: XMM/YMM register file and vector lanes
Intrinsics as direct machine instruction mappings
Page boundaries and safe memory access

milestones.title: SSE2 Basics: memset and memcpy
milestones.summary: Master the fundamentals of SSE2 intrinsics by implementing vectorized memory operations. Learn the scalar prologue/epilogue pattern for handling unaligned edges, compare aligned vs unaligned loads/stores, and discover when non-temporal stores help (and when they hurt).
milestones.misconception: Developers assume memcpy is just 'copy bytes in a loop' and that SIMD makes it faster by copying more bytes per iteration. They also believe memset/memcpy are simple functions that the OS provides.
milestones.reveal: libc's memset and memcpy are already heavily SIMD-optimized with AVX-512 on modern CPUs, non-temporal stores for large buffers, and sophisticated alignment handling. Your hand-written SSE2 may actually be slower than libc! The real learning is understanding WHY: alignment constraints, cache behavior, and the instruction mix. Also, memcpy touches every cache line, which can evict useful data—for buffers larger than L2 cache, non-temporal (streaming) stores bypass the cache entirely.
cascade: Memory alignment is a hardware constraint, not a software preference — misaligned access either faults (ARM) or suffers 2-3x latency penalty (x86)
Cache hierarchy awareness (L1/L2/L3 sizes) determines when non-temporal stores win — evicting your own working set is a classic performance bug (cross-domain: database buffer pool sizing)
Compiler auto-vectorization already does this — but only when it can prove alignment and absence of aliasing (leads to M4 analysis)
Prefetching and cache line behavior — sequential 16-byte stores trigger hardware prefetchers, random patterns don't (cross-domain: why linked lists lose to arrays)
Bandwidth vs latency thinking — SIMD improves throughput but each load/store still has the same latency; this matters for dependency chains
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Implement simd_memset using _mm_store_si128 processing 16 bytes per iteration for aligned regions
Implement simd_memcpy using _mm_load_si128 / _mm_store_si128 pairs for aligned 16-byte copies
Handle unaligned buffer edges with a scalar prologue (process bytes until 16-byte aligned) and scalar epilogue (process remaining bytes after last full 16-byte chunk)
For buffers smaller than 16 bytes, fall back entirely to scalar implementation
Benchmark against libc memset/memcpy and scalar loop across buffer sizes (64B, 1KB, 64KB, 1MB, 16MB)
Show measurable speedup over naive scalar loop for buffers >= 1KB; explain results where no speedup is observed (libc is already SIMD-optimized)
Use _mm_stream_si128 (non-temporal store) for buffers larger than L2 cache and benchmark the difference

milestones.title: String Operations: strlen and memchr
milestones.summary: Implement parallel byte comparison for string scanning. The critical challenge is page-boundary safety: a 16-byte read starting at address 0x4FFF would access 0x500F, potentially crossing into unmapped memory. Learn the aligned-read-with-masking technique used by production libc implementations.
milestones.misconception: Developers think strlen just 'reads bytes until null' and that SIMD can speed it up by checking 16 bytes at once. They assume memory reads are always safe within the process address space.
milestones.reveal: Reading 16 bytes starting at an arbitrary address can segfault even if the string itself is valid! If your read crosses a page boundary into unmapped memory, the CPU faults. The solution is profound: always read from 16-byte aligned addresses, and use comparison masking to ignore bytes before the string start. This pattern—aligned access plus bit masking—is a fundamental SIMD technique reused everywhere.
cascade: Page granularity (4KB) and page fault mechanics — the OS maps memory at page granularity, and a read that straddles a page boundary can fault even if only 1 byte is unmapped (cross-domain: why direct I/O requires page-aligned buffers)
movemask as a general pattern — extracting comparison results into an integer enables fast bit-scanning (__builtin_ctz) and parallel decision-making

Branch-free programming — SIMD comparisons produce bitmasks, not branches; this enables data-dependent logic without branch mispredictions (cross-domain: how databases evaluate WHERE clauses on columnar data)
Libc implementations use this exact pattern — glibc's strlen uses SSE2 with the aligned-read technique; studying real code reveals production techniques
Security implications — reading past buffer bounds can leak data (Spectre variant); page-safe reads prevent information leakage
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Implement simd_strlen using _mm_cmpeq_epi8 to compare 16 bytes against zero simultaneously
Implement simd_memchr using _mm_cmpeq_epi8 to find a target byte across 16 bytes simultaneously
Use _mm_movemask_epi8 to convert comparison results to a 16-bit bitmask; use __builtin_ctz (count trailing zeros) to find the first match position

Handle page-boundary safety: before the first aligned read, compute the distance from the input pointer to the next page boundary (4096-byte aligned). If the input is within 16 bytes of a page boundary, use scalar scanning until aligned. After alignment, 16-byte aligned reads within a page cannot fault.
For the initial unaligned chunk (pointer not 16-byte aligned), either use scalar scanning OR read from the aligned-down address and mask out bytes before the actual start
Verify correctness with strings of lengths 0, 1, 15, 16, 17, 4095, 4096, 4097 (page boundary cases)
Benchmark against libc strlen/memchr across string lengths

milestones.title: Math Operations: Dot Product and Matrix Multiply
milestones.summary: Apply SIMD to floating-point computation with horizontal reductions. Discover why _mm_hadd_ps is often the wrong choice, how data layout (row-major vs column-major) affects vectorization, and how to handle AVX-SSE transition penalties. Implement runtime CPU feature detection for portable code.
milestones.misconception: Developers believe SIMD floating-point is just 'replace scalar ops with vector ops' and that horizontal add (_mm_hadd_ps) is the right way to sum vector elements. They also assume row-major matrix storage is always fine.
milestones.reveal: _mm_hadd_ps has 3-5 cycle latency and poor throughput on many Intel CPUs. The shuffle+vertical-add pattern (shuffle low-high, add pairs, repeat) is 2-3x faster for horizontal reduction. For matrices, row-major storage means column access is non-contiguous, defeating SIMD and causing cache misses. Column-major or blocked layouts enable vectorized column operations. Also, mixing SSE and AVX code without vzeroupper causes a 70-cycle penalty on older Intel CPUs—the upper 128 bits of YMM registers become undefined.
cascade: Instruction latency vs throughput — hadd has high latency but the real issue is port contention; shuffle+add uses different execution ports and can overlap (cross-domain: understanding database query plan execution)
Data layout transforms algorithms — matrix multiply is O(n³) regardless of layout, but cache behavior makes blocked/column-major 10x faster (cross-domain: column-oriented databases vs row-oriented)
Horizontal reduction is a general pattern — summing a vector appears in reduction operations everywhere: dot products, sum aggregations, softmax, attention mechanisms in transformers

AVX-SSE transition penalty reveals microarchitectural state — YMM registers alias XMM, and the CPU tracks 'dirty' upper halves; vzeroupper is a hint, not a functional requirement (cross-domain: hypervisor world-switches have similar state-save costs)
Runtime dispatch enables portable binaries — CPUID detection + function pointers let you ship one binary that uses AVX-512 on new CPUs and SSE2 on old ones
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Implement SIMD dot product using _mm_mul_ps for element-wise multiply, then reduce using shuffle+add pattern (_mm_shuffle_ps + _mm_add_ps), NOT _mm_hadd_ps
Implement 4x4 float matrix multiplication using SSE, processing one row-column dot product per iteration
Support both row-major and column-major matrix layouts; benchmark both to demonstrate access pattern impact
Implement AVX (256-bit) variants of both operations using _mm256_* intrinsics
Insert _mm256_zeroupper() or use __attribute__((target("avx"))) to avoid AVX-SSE transition penalties when mixing code paths
Implement runtime CPU feature detection using CPUID (or compiler builtins) to select SSE or AVX code path
Benchmark scalar vs SSE vs AVX across array sizes (4, 16, 64, 256, 1024 elements for dot product; various matrix sizes)
Show at least 2x speedup for SSE and 3x for AVX over scalar for large inputs (1024+ elements)

milestones.title: Auto-vectorization Analysis
milestones.summary: Step back and analyze what compilers actually do. Write scalar code structured for auto-vectorization, study compiler vectorization reports, inspect generated assembly, and discover the cases where hand-written SIMD wins—and where the compiler matches or beats you. Learn rigorous benchmarking methodology.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume compilers are magic and will vectorize any simple loop, OR that hand-written SIMD is always faster. They also believe benchmarking is just 'measure average time over a few runs.'
milestones.reveal: Compilers are extremely conservative about vectorization because it must preserve exact semantics. Pointer aliasing (can dst[i] overlap src[i]?) prevents vectorization unless you use restrict. Floating-point reassociation (a+b+c ≠ a+(b+c) in IEEE 754) blocks FP vectorization without -ffast-math. However, when the compiler CAN vectorize, it often produces code matching hand-written intrinsics—the compiler knows instruction latencies for your specific CPU. Hand-written SIMD wins when: (1) the compiler can't prove safety, (2) complex control flow, (3) you know alignment the compiler doesn't. Rigorous benchmarking requires CPU frequency pinning, cache warmup, and statistical analysis—untimed warmup runs eliminate cold-cache outliers.
cascade: Pointer aliasing analysis is NP-hard — the compiler must prove no overlap; restrict keyword and __builtin_assume_aligned are hints (cross-domain: database query optimization faces similar dependency analysis)

Floating-point determinism vs performance — -ffast-math enables vectorization but changes numerical results; this is unacceptable for scientific computing but fine for games (cross-domain: ML training uses mixed precision for speed)
Compiler optimization is an active research area — newer compiler versions vectorize more aggressively; your hand-written code may be optimal today but suboptimal on future CPUs

Statistical rigor in benchmarking — median is robust to outliers, standard deviation reveals measurement noise, frequency pinning eliminates turbo-boost variance (cross-domain: A/B testing in product analytics requires similar rigor)
Reading assembly is a superpower — even if you never write assembly, understanding what the compiler emits lets you diagnose performance mysteries
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Write scalar versions of all functions structured for auto-vectorization (simple loops, no pointer aliasing, use restrict keyword)
Compile with -O3 -march=native -ftree-vectorize -fopt-info-vec-all (GCC) or -Rpass=loop-vectorize (Clang) and capture vectorization reports
Inspect generated assembly for at least 3 functions; annotate which SIMD instructions the compiler chose

Identify at least 2 cases where hand-written SIMD outperforms auto-vectorization and explain why (e.g., the compiler couldn't prove alignment, couldn't vectorize complex control flow, chose suboptimal instruction sequence)
Identify at least 1 case where auto-vectorization matches or beats hand-written SIMD and explain why
Benchmark methodology: pin CPU frequency (disable turbo boost and frequency scaling), warm up caches with 3 untimed runs, report median of 10+ timed runs with standard deviation
Disable auto-vectorization for scalar baselines using __attribute__((optimize("no-tree-vectorize"))) or equivalent
Produce a written analysis document (markdown) summarizing findings with assembly excerpts and benchmark data

diagrams.title: SIMD Optimization Library: System Map
diagrams.description: Satellite view of the entire project. Shows the four milestones as interconnected regions with their key concepts. Central hub is the XMM/YMM register file, with arrows to M1 (memory ops), M2 (string scanning), M3 (math ops), and M4 (compiler analysis). Each region shows data flow: M1 takes src/dst buffers and produces copied/initialized memory; M2 takes strings and produces positions; M3 takes arrays/matrices and produces scalar results; M4 shows the compiler decision tree. Includes cache hierarchy (L1→L2→L3→RAM) on the side affecting all operations.

diagrams.title: XMM Register: 128-bit Vector Lane Structure
diagrams.description: Microscopic view of a single XMM register. Shows 128 bits divided into 16 byte lanes (lane 0-15), 8 word lanes, 4 dword/float lanes, and 2 qword/double lanes. Each lane labeled with bit positions. Below shows _mm_set_epi8(15,14,...,0) mapping to lane positions. Critical for understanding why _mm_cmpeq_epi8 produces 16 parallel comparisons.

diagrams.title: SIMD memcpy: Data Flow Through Memory Hierarchy
diagrams.description: Data walk diagram showing src buffer → L1 cache → XMM register (via _mm_load_si128) → L1 cache → dst buffer (via _mm_store_si128). Shows 16-byte chunks flowing through the pipeline with cache line boundaries marked. Below shows the prologue (1-15 bytes scalar), main loop (aligned 16-byte chunks), and epilogue (1-15 bytes scalar).

diagrams.title: Alignment Handling: Prologue and Epilogue Pattern
diagrams.description: Structure layout showing a 47-byte buffer at address 0x1003. Shows: (1) prologue processes bytes 0x1003-0x100F (13 bytes) to reach 16-byte alignment at 0x1010, (2) main loop processes 0x1010-0x102F (32 bytes = 2 full vectors), (3) epilogue processes 0x1030-0x1031 (2 remaining bytes). Uses arrows and color coding to show scalar (orange) vs SIMD (blue) regions.

diagrams.title: Non-Temporal Stores: Cache Hierarchy Impact
diagrams.description: Before/After comparison. Before: normal _mm_store_si128 writes through L1→L2→L3, causing eviction of other data. After: _mm_stream_si128 bypasses cache entirely, going straight to memory. Shows L2 cache size boundary (typically 256KB-1MB) and when to switch to streaming stores. Includes bandwidth graph showing NT stores winning above L2 size.

diagrams.title: Benchmark Results: SIMD vs Scalar vs libc
diagrams.description: Graph template showing throughput (GB/s) vs buffer size (64B to 16MB, log scale). Three lines: naive scalar loop, hand-written SSE2, libc memset/memcpy. Expected result: libc wins or matches, SSE2 beats naive scalar for >1KB. Annotated regions show where each approach wins.

diagrams.title: Page Boundary Hazard: Why Unaligned Reads Fault
diagrams.description: Memory map showing two 4KB pages (Page 1: 0x4000-0x4FFF, Page 2: 0x5000-0x5FFF). Page 1 is mapped (green), Page 2 is unmapped (red). Shows a 16-byte read starting at 0x4FF0 crossing into Page 2 at 0x5000—this causes SIGSEGV. Below shows the safe approach: read from aligned address 0x4FF0 is fine (fully within Page 1), but read from 0x4FF8 would cross.

diagrams.title: Aligned Read with Byte Masking
diagrams.description: Step-by-step trace. Input string 'hello\0' at address 0x1003. Step 1: Align down to 0x1000. Step 2: Load 16 bytes from 0x1000 (includes garbage bytes 0x1000-0x1002). Step 3: Create mask for bytes ≥ 0x1003. Step 4: Compare all 16 bytes against '\0'. Step 5: Apply mask to movemask result. Shows bitmask values at each step.

diagrams.title: From Comparison to Position: movemask + ctz
diagrams.description: Horizontal flow showing _mm_cmpeq_epi8 result (16 bytes of 0x00 or 0xFF) → _mm_movemask_epi8 (16-bit integer) → __builtin_ctz (index of first set bit). Example: string 'abcd\0efgh...' produces comparison [0,0,0,0,0xFF,0,0,...], movemask = 0x0010, ctz(0x0010) = 4. Shows why this is O(1) position finding.

diagrams.title: SIMD strlen: State Evolution
diagrams.description: State evolution diagram. State 0: Check distance to page boundary. State 1: If <16 bytes to boundary, scalar scan until aligned. State 2: Load 16 aligned bytes. State 3: Compare against zero. State 4: If mask != 0, compute position and return. State 5: If mask == 0, advance pointer by 16, goto State 2. Shows loop structure clearly.

diagrams.title: String Length Test Coverage
diagrams.description: Matrix showing test cases (lengths 0, 1, 15, 16, 17, 4095, 4096, 4097) vs properties (within first chunk, spans chunks, at page boundary, crosses page boundary). Each cell shows expected behavior. Critical for understanding why these specific lengths are chosen.

diagrams.title: SIMD Dot Product: Element-wise Multiply Then Reduce
diagrams.description: Data walk showing two input arrays [a0,a1,a2,a3,...] and [b0,b1,b2,b3,...] loading into XMM registers, element-wise multiply producing [a0*b0, a1*b1, a2*b2, a3*b3], then the reduction sequence. Shows accumulator register accumulating partial sums.

diagrams.title: Horizontal Reduction: hadd vs Shuffle+Add
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison. Left: _mm_hadd_ps path showing 3-5 cycle latency per hadd, requiring 2 hadd calls to reduce 4 floats. Right: shuffle+add path showing _mm_shuffle_ps to swap halves, _mm_add_ps to combine, then _mm_shuffle_ps + _mm_add_ss for final reduction. Shows cycle counts and port usage for Intel Skylake.

diagrams.title: Shuffle+Add Reduction: Step by Step
diagrams.description: Microscopic trace. Initial vector [s0, s1, s2, s3]. Step 1: Shuffle to get [s2, s3, s0, s1]. Step 2: Add to get [s0+s2, s1+s3, s2+s0, s3+s1]. Step 3: Shuffle to get [s1+s3, s0+s2, ...]. Step 4: Add low elements to get final sum. Shows exactly which lanes combine at each step.

diagrams.title: Matrix Layout: Row-Major vs Column-Major Access
diagrams.description: Structure layout comparison. 4x4 matrix shown in row-major (rows contiguous) and column-major (columns contiguous). For matrix multiply C = A×B, shows access pattern: A's rows × B's columns. Row-major A + column-major B = both contiguous. Row-major A + row-major B = B access is strided (cache hostile). Includes cache line overlay showing which elements share a line.

diagrams.title: AVX YMM Register: 256-bit Extension
diagrams.description: Register diagram showing YMM0 as 256 bits (YMM0[255:128] upper half, YMM0[127:0] lower half = XMM0). Shows 8 float lanes or 8 dword lanes. Below shows _mm256_mul_ps processing 8 floats per instruction vs _mm_mul_ps processing 4. Also shows aliasing: accessing XMM0 clears upper 128 bits of YMM0 (requires vzeroupper context).

diagrams.title: AVX-SSE Transition Penalty: The vzeroupper Problem
diagrams.description: Before/After timeline. Before (no vzeroupper): AVX code writes YMM register, SSE code reads XMM (upper 128 bits undefined), CPU detects state mismatch, 70-cycle penalty to save/restore upper bits. After (with vzeroupper): AVX code followed by vzeroupper, SSE code runs cleanly. Shows the state machine the CPU uses to track 'dirty' upper halves.

diagrams.title: Runtime CPU Feature Detection Flow
diagrams.description: Decision tree for CPUID detection. Start: Call __get_cpuid(1) for basic features. Check EDX bit 26 for SSE2. Check ECX bit 28 for AVX. If AVX, call __get_cpuid_count(7, 0) for AVX2, AVX-512. Returns feature flags struct. Shows function pointer dispatch: init_simd() sets function pointers to best available implementation.

diagrams.title: Benchmark Results: Scalar vs SSE vs AVX
diagrams.description: Bar chart showing speedup (relative to scalar=1.0) for dot product at sizes 4, 16, 64, 256, 1024 elements. Three bars per size: scalar (baseline), SSE (~2-3x at 1024), AVX (~3-4x at 1024). Shows diminishing returns at small sizes (overhead dominates). Target line at 2x for SSE and 3x for AVX.

diagrams.title: Compiler Vectorization Decision Tree
diagrams.description: Decision tree showing compiler's internal analysis. Start: Is loop count known? → No: try with runtime checks. → Yes: Is iteration count >= vector width? → No: don't vectorize. → Yes: Any function calls? → Yes: only if known safe. → No: Pointer aliasing possible? → Yes: require runtime checks or skip. → No: FP reassociation needed? → Yes: require -ffast-math. → No: VECTORIZE. Shows why simple loops vectorize and complex ones don't.

diagrams.title: Pointer Aliasing: What the Compiler Must Prove
diagrams.description: Code examples side by side. Left: void add(float* a, float* b, float* c, int n) { for(...) c[i] = a[i] + b[i]; } — compiler can't prove a, b, c don't overlap, may not vectorize. Right: same with restrict keyword — compiler assumes no overlap, vectorizes. Shows generated assembly for both (scalar vs vector).

diagrams.title: Assembly Annotation: Reading Compiler Output
diagrams.description: Example annotated assembly for simd_dot_product. Shows movaps (aligned load), mulps (parallel multiply), addps (parallel add), and shuffle/add reduction sequence. Each instruction annotated with latency, throughput, and port. Comments explain what each instruction does in terms of the original C code.

diagrams.title: Hand-Written vs Auto-Vectorized: When Each Wins
diagrams.description: Comparison matrix. Rows: different code patterns (aligned arrays, unaligned input, complex control flow, horizontal reduction, page-boundary safety). Columns: hand-written SIMD result, auto-vectorized result, winner. Shows hand-written wins for page safety, complex control flow; auto wins for simple loops with known alignment; tie for basic arithmetic.

diagrams.title: Rigorous Benchmarking: Methodology Checklist
diagrams.description: Flowchart/checklist hybrid. Pre-benchmark: disable turbo boost (cpupower frequency-set -g performance), disable hyperthreading, clear cache (sync; echo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches). Warmup: 3 untimed runs to populate cache. Measurement: median of 10+ runs, record stddev. Post-processing: detect outliers (>2 stddev), verify CPU frequency stayed constant. Shows common mistakes and their symptoms.

diagrams.title: -ffast-math: Performance vs Correctness Trade-offs
diagrams.description: Before/After comparison. Without -ffast-math: IEEE 754 exact semantics, (a+b)+c ≠ a+(b+c) due to rounding, compiler won't reorder FP ops, no vectorization of reductions. With -ffast-math: reassociation allowed, vectorization enabled, but numerical results may differ, NaN/infinity handling changes, not suitable for scientific computing. Shows benchmark speedup but also shows counter-example where results diverge.

diagrams.title: Hardware Soul: Cache Line Boundaries in SIMD
diagrams.description: Memory diagram showing 64-byte cache lines. A 16-byte SIMD load from address 0x38 loads from cache line 0x00-0x3F (hit). A load from 0x3C loads bytes 0x3C-0x4B, spanning TWO cache lines (0x00-0x3F and 0x40-0x7F). Shows L1 cache structure and how spanning loads have slightly higher latency. Critical for understanding why 32-byte and 64-byte alignments matter for AVX/AVX-512.

diagrams.title: Hardware Soul: Execution Ports for SIMD Instructions
diagrams.description: CPU execution engine diagram showing ports 0-5. Port 0: mulps, addps (FMA). Port 1: addps, shuffle. Port 5: shuffle, movemask. Shows how shuffle+add can execute in parallel (different ports) while hadd may serialize. Includes throughput table for common SIMD instructions on Skylake/Ice Lake.

diagrams.title: Cross-Domain: SIMD in Database Query Processing
diagrams.description: Shows how database column stores use SIMD for WHERE clause evaluation. Table with 'age' column [25, 30, 45, 18, ...] loaded into XMM register, compared against 30 in parallel, producing bitmask [0, 1, 1, 0, ...]. This is the same movemask pattern from strlen applied to columnar data processing. Links back to M2 concepts.

diagrams.title: Cross-Domain: SIMD in Transformer Attention
diagrams.description: Shows attention mechanism Q×K^T using matrix multiply (from M3), softmax requiring horizontal reduction (from M3), and batch processing of multiple attention heads using SIMD lanes. Demonstrates that understanding SIMD horizontal reduction directly explains why attention is compute-bound.

diagrams_to_generate.title: SIMD Optimization Library: System Map
diagrams_to_generate.description: Satellite view of the entire project. Shows the four milestones as interconnected regions with their key concepts. Central hub is the XMM/YMM register file, with arrows to M1 (memory ops), M2 (string scanning), M3 (math ops), and M4 (compiler analysis). Each region shows data flow: M1 takes src/dst buffers and produces copied/initialized memory; M2 takes strings and produces positions; M3 takes arrays/matrices and produces scalar results; M4 shows the compiler decision tree. Includes cache hierarchy (L1→L2→L3→RAM) on the side affecting all operations.

diagrams_to_generate.title: XMM Register: 128-bit Vector Lane Structure
diagrams_to_generate.description: Microscopic view of a single XMM register. Shows 128 bits divided into 16 byte lanes (lane 0-15), 8 word lanes, 4 dword/float lanes, and 2 qword/double lanes. Each lane labeled with bit positions. Below shows _mm_set_epi8(15,14,...,0) mapping to lane positions. Critical for understanding why _mm_cmpeq_epi8 produces 16 parallel comparisons.

diagrams_to_generate.title: SIMD memcpy: Data Flow Through Memory Hierarchy
diagrams_to_generate.description: Data walk diagram showing src buffer → L1 cache → XMM register (via _mm_load_si128) → L1 cache → dst buffer (via _mm_store_si128). Shows 16-byte chunks flowing through the pipeline with cache line boundaries marked. Below shows the prologue (1-15 bytes scalar), main loop (aligned 16-byte chunks), and epilogue (1-15 bytes scalar).

diagrams_to_generate.title: Alignment Handling: Prologue and Epilogue Pattern
diagrams_to_generate.description: Structure layout showing a 47-byte buffer at address 0x1003. Shows: (1) prologue processes bytes 0x1003-0x100F (13 bytes) to reach 16-byte alignment at 0x1010, (2) main loop processes 0x1010-0x102F (32 bytes = 2 full vectors), (3) epilogue processes 0x1030-0x1031 (2 remaining bytes). Uses arrows and color coding to show scalar (orange) vs SIMD (blue) regions.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Non-Temporal Stores: Cache Hierarchy Impact
diagrams_to_generate.description: Before/After comparison. Before: normal _mm_store_si128 writes through L1→L2→L3, causing eviction of other data. After: _mm_stream_si128 bypasses cache entirely, going straight to memory. Shows L2 cache size boundary (typically 256KB-1MB) and when to switch to streaming stores. Includes bandwidth graph showing NT stores winning above L2 size.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Benchmark Results: SIMD vs Scalar vs libc
diagrams_to_generate.description: Graph template showing throughput (GB/s) vs buffer size (64B to 16MB, log scale). Three lines: naive scalar loop, hand-written SSE2, libc memset/memcpy. Expected result: libc wins or matches, SSE2 beats naive scalar for >1KB. Annotated regions show where each approach wins.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Page Boundary Hazard: Why Unaligned Reads Fault
diagrams_to_generate.description: Memory map showing two 4KB pages (Page 1: 0x4000-0x4FFF, Page 2: 0x5000-0x5FFF). Page 1 is mapped (green), Page 2 is unmapped (red). Shows a 16-byte read starting at 0x4FF0 crossing into Page 2 at 0x5000—this causes SIGSEGV. Below shows the safe approach: read from aligned address 0x4FF0 is fine (fully within Page 1), but read from 0x4FF8 would cross.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Aligned Read with Byte Masking
diagrams_to_generate.description: Step-by-step trace. Input string 'hello\0' at address 0x1003. Step 1: Align down to 0x1000. Step 2: Load 16 bytes from 0x1000 (includes garbage bytes 0x1000-0x1002). Step 3: Create mask for bytes ≥ 0x1003. Step 4: Compare all 16 bytes against '\0'. Step 5: Apply mask to movemask result. Shows bitmask values at each step.

diagrams_to_generate.title: From Comparison to Position: movemask + ctz
diagrams_to_generate.description: Horizontal flow showing _mm_cmpeq_epi8 result (16 bytes of 0x00 or 0xFF) → _mm_movemask_epi8 (16-bit integer) → __builtin_ctz (index of first set bit). Example: string 'abcd\0efgh...' produces comparison [0,0,0,0,0xFF,0,0,...], movemask = 0x0010, ctz(0x0010) = 4. Shows why this is O(1) position finding.

diagrams_to_generate.title: SIMD strlen: State Evolution
diagrams_to_generate.description: State evolution diagram. State 0: Check distance to page boundary. State 1: If <16 bytes to boundary, scalar scan until aligned. State 2: Load 16 aligned bytes. State 3: Compare against zero. State 4: If mask != 0, compute position and return. State 5: If mask == 0, advance pointer by 16, goto State 2. Shows loop structure clearly.

diagrams_to_generate.title: String Length Test Coverage
diagrams_to_generate.description: Matrix showing test cases (lengths 0, 1, 15, 16, 17, 4095, 4096, 4097) vs properties (within first chunk, spans chunks, at page boundary, crosses page boundary). Each cell shows expected behavior. Critical for understanding why these specific lengths are chosen.

diagrams_to_generate.title: SIMD Dot Product: Element-wise Multiply Then Reduce
diagrams_to_generate.description: Data walk showing two input arrays [a0,a1,a2,a3,...] and [b0,b1,b2,b3,...] loading into XMM registers, element-wise multiply producing [a0*b0, a1*b1, a2*b2, a3*b3], then the reduction sequence. Shows accumulator register accumulating partial sums.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Horizontal Reduction: hadd vs Shuffle+Add
diagrams_to_generate.description: Side-by-side comparison. Left: _mm_hadd_ps path showing 3-5 cycle latency per hadd, requiring 2 hadd calls to reduce 4 floats. Right: shuffle+add path showing _mm_shuffle_ps to swap halves, _mm_add_ps to combine, then _mm_shuffle_ps + _mm_add_ss for final reduction. Shows cycle counts and port usage for Intel Skylake.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Shuffle+Add Reduction: Step by Step
diagrams_to_generate.description: Microscopic trace. Initial vector [s0, s1, s2, s3]. Step 1: Shuffle to get [s2, s3, s0, s1]. Step 2: Add to get [s0+s2, s1+s3, s2+s0, s3+s1]. Step 3: Shuffle to get [s1+s3, s0+s2, ...]. Step 4: Add low elements to get final sum. Shows exactly which lanes combine at each step.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Matrix Layout: Row-Major vs Column-Major Access
diagrams_to_generate.description: Structure layout comparison. 4x4 matrix shown in row-major (rows contiguous) and column-major (columns contiguous). For matrix multiply C = A×B, shows access pattern: A's rows × B's columns. Row-major A + column-major B = both contiguous. Row-major A + row-major B = B access is strided (cache hostile). Includes cache line overlay showing which elements share a line.

diagrams_to_generate.title: AVX YMM Register: 256-bit Extension
diagrams_to_generate.description: Register diagram showing YMM0 as 256 bits (YMM0[255:128] upper half, YMM0[127:0] lower half = XMM0). Shows 8 float lanes or 8 dword lanes. Below shows _mm256_mul_ps processing 8 floats per instruction vs _mm_mul_ps processing 4. Also shows aliasing: accessing XMM0 clears upper 128 bits of YMM0 (requires vzeroupper context).

diagrams_to_generate.title: AVX-SSE Transition Penalty: The vzeroupper Problem
diagrams_to_generate.description: Before/After timeline. Before (no vzeroupper): AVX code writes YMM register, SSE code reads XMM (upper 128 bits undefined), CPU detects state mismatch, 70-cycle penalty to save/restore upper bits. After (with vzeroupper): AVX code followed by vzeroupper, SSE code runs cleanly. Shows the state machine the CPU uses to track 'dirty' upper halves.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Runtime CPU Feature Detection Flow
diagrams_to_generate.description: Decision tree for CPUID detection. Start: Call __get_cpuid(1) for basic features. Check EDX bit 26 for SSE2. Check ECX bit 28 for AVX. If AVX, call __get_cpuid_count(7, 0) for AVX2, AVX-512. Returns feature flags struct. Shows function pointer dispatch: init_simd() sets function pointers to best available implementation.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Benchmark Results: Scalar vs SSE vs AVX
diagrams_to_generate.description: Bar chart showing speedup (relative to scalar=1.0) for dot product at sizes 4, 16, 64, 256, 1024 elements. Three bars per size: scalar (baseline), SSE (~2-3x at 1024), AVX (~3-4x at 1024). Shows diminishing returns at small sizes (overhead dominates). Target line at 2x for SSE and 3x for AVX.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Compiler Vectorization Decision Tree
diagrams_to_generate.description: Decision tree showing compiler's internal analysis. Start: Is loop count known? → No: try with runtime checks. → Yes: Is iteration count >= vector width? → No: don't vectorize. → Yes: Any function calls? → Yes: only if known safe. → No: Pointer aliasing possible? → Yes: require runtime checks or skip. → No: FP reassociation needed? → Yes: require -ffast-math. → No: VECTORIZE. Shows why simple loops vectorize and complex ones don't.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Pointer Aliasing: What the Compiler Must Prove
diagrams_to_generate.description: Code examples side by side. Left: void add(float* a, float* b, float* c, int n) { for(...) c[i] = a[i] + b[i]; } — compiler can't prove a, b, c don't overlap, may not vectorize. Right: same with restrict keyword — compiler assumes no overlap, vectorizes. Shows generated assembly for both (scalar vs vector).

diagrams_to_generate.title: Assembly Annotation: Reading Compiler Output
diagrams_to_generate.description: Example annotated assembly for simd_dot_product. Shows movaps (aligned load), mulps (parallel multiply), addps (parallel add), and shuffle/add reduction sequence. Each instruction annotated with latency, throughput, and port. Comments explain what each instruction does in terms of the original C code.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Hand-Written vs Auto-Vectorized: When Each Wins
diagrams_to_generate.description: Comparison matrix. Rows: different code patterns (aligned arrays, unaligned input, complex control flow, horizontal reduction, page-boundary safety). Columns: hand-written SIMD result, auto-vectorized result, winner. Shows hand-written wins for page safety, complex control flow; auto wins for simple loops with known alignment; tie for basic arithmetic.

diagrams_to_generate.description: Flowchart/checklist hybrid. With -ffast-math: reassociation allowed, vectorization enabled, but numerical results may differ, NaN/infinity handling changes, not suitable for scientific computing. Shows benchmark speedup but also shows counter-example where results diverge.

Shows how shuffle+add can execute in parallel (different ports) while hadd may serialize. Includes throughput table for common SIMD instructions on Skylake/Ice Lake.

criteria: Implement simd_memset using _mm_set1_epi8 to create fill pattern and _mm_store_si128 for aligned 16-byte stores
Implement simd_memcpy using _mm_loadu_si128/_mm_store_si128 pairs with destination alignment
Handle buffer edges with scalar prologue (align to 16-byte boundary) and scalar epilogue (remaining 0-15 bytes)
Fall back to pure scalar implementation for buffers smaller than 16 bytes
Implement simd_memset_stream variant using _mm_stream_si128 with _mm_sfence fence
Benchmark scalar, simd_memset, simd_memcpy, and libc memset/memcpy across sizes (64B, 1KB, 64KB, 1MB, 16MB)
Disable compiler auto-vectorization for scalar baseline using __attribute__((optimize("no-tree-vectorize")))
Document benchmark methodology: CPU frequency pinning, warmup runs, median of 10+ iterations
Explain why libc may outperform hand-written SSE2 (AVX/AVX-512 usage, non-temporal stores, CPU-specific tuning)
Identify buffer size threshold where non-temporal stores provide measurable benefit vs standard stores
